items.title: Elon Musk confirms Grok 4.5 goes public tomorrow (July 9) — the first jointly-built SpaceXAI-and-Cursor frontier model, a 1.5-trillion-parameter V9 architecture trained from scratch on SpaceX's ~1M-H100-equivalent Colossus using Cursor engineers' code as training data, shipping simultaneously inside Cursor the IDE and inside Grok Build the terminal-agent CLI; Musk's pitch: 'Opus-class capability, faster, more token-efficient, lower cost' — the first substantive test of whether an integrated compute-plus-distribution-plus-training-data stack outperforms the frontier-lab model
items.summary: Elon Musk announced on X on July 8, 2026 that Grok 4.5 — the first joint SpaceXAI + Cursor model — will be released to the public tomorrow, July 9, following what he called 'strong positive feedback from beta customers.' Multiple sources over the past two days (The Information, AI Weekly, Reuters, The Next Web, ExplainX, DigitalApplied, testingcatalog, gizmodo, FourWeekMBA, TradingKey) confirm the specifics. The model is built on a brand-new 1.5-trillion-parameter V9 foundation architecture — not a fine-tune of prior Grok — trained from scratch on SpaceX's Colossus supercomputer, which scales to roughly one million H100-equivalent GPUs. Training data includes supplemental code from Cursor's active engineer base. The model ships simultaneously inside Cursor (the AI code editor) and inside Grok Build (SpaceXAI's terminal-based agentic CLI, ~$30/month via SuperGrok, currently scored at 70.8% on SWE-bench Verified as of May 15). Grok Build runs up to 8 agents in parallel through a plan → search → build workflow, via TUI, headless scripts, or the Agent Client Protocol. Musk's stated targeting: Anthropic's Opus 4.8 and OpenAI's GPT-5.5 (note: GPT-5.5, not GPT-5.6 Sol — the internal memo cited by The Information predates the July 7 GPT-5.6 pricing). Context: SpaceX closed the $60B all-stock Cursor acquisition on June 16, 2026 (CNBC/GovConWire), then folded xAI into SpaceX and rebranded as SpaceXAI in early July. The launch was originally slated for earlier this week but pushed back to improve efficiency. Model details (context window, specific benchmark numbers, pricing per token) have not been made public as of this snapshot.
items.why_it_matters: This is the substantive Tier-1 story of the week for Gabe. Two threads worth understanding. First, the practical read on tool choice. If you use Cursor for Python work, tomorrow the default model behind Cursor's autocomplete and agent flows becomes a frontier model that Cursor's parent company owns end-to-end — not a Claude or GPT wrapper. Musk's 'faster, more token-efficient, lower cost' framing points at the specific pain point of the last three months (frontier model calls getting expensive, subscription tiers hitting weekly caps — see the Fable 5 extension and GPT-5.6 pricing items in this morning's am file). If Grok 4.5 delivers on that pitch, Cursor becomes the cheapest frontier-in-the-loop coding experience for a hobbyist Python builder, and the calculus 'do I use Cursor or Claude Code' gets a real re-open. Second, the structural read on the industry. Until this week the frontier model market has been three labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) plus one open-weight tier (Mistral, Meta, Z.ai). Grok 4.5 is the first frontier model where the SAME entity owns the compute (Colossus, ~1M H100), the training data pipeline (Cursor engineers writing code all day), the model (V9 1.5T from scratch), and the distribution surface (Cursor IDE + Grok Build CLI). That's the fully vertically-integrated stack the AI industry has been talking about since ChatGPT launched — this is the first concrete implementation. Whether the resulting model is actually competitive at frontier is the specific question July 9 answers. Practical note: if you're on a Cursor subscription, watch your inbox for pricing/tier changes tomorrow — a new default frontier model usually means the free-tier / Pro-tier quota math changes with it. The 1.5T-parameter scale is roughly in line with what Anthropic and OpenAI are believed to run for their flagship tiers, so this is a genuine frontier-scale deployment, not a smaller specialized coder.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Cursor + SpaceXAI ship the first jointly-built frontier model tomorrow — 1.5T params, trained from scratch on Colossus, distributed through Cursor and Grok Build — the first fully vertically-integrated frontier stack (compute + data + model + distribution owned end-to-end).

items.title: OpenAI confirms GPT-5.6 Sol / Terra / Luna go public globally on Thursday, July 9 — US Department of Commerce cleared the launch after completing the government security review OpenAI submitted on June 26, lifting the two-week restricted-preview limit that had capped access to ~20 'trusted partners' at White House request; Sam Altman is publicly positioning the government-limits framework as something that 'shouldn't be the norm'
items.summary: OpenAI announced July 8, 2026 (CNBC, Bloomberg, PYMNTS, TechCrunch, ZeroHedge, TechBuzz, Cyberpress) that GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna will be publicly released Thursday, July 9, ending the two-week government-requested restricted preview that had limited access to roughly 20 trusted partner organizations since June 26. The US Department of Commerce completed its security review and cleared OpenAI for the broad public rollout; the White House formally approved the launch this week. OpenAI is also expanding preview access to the models globally simultaneously with the US public release. Positioning of the three tiers: Sol is the flagship — OpenAI's 'strongest model yet in terms of agentic capabilities in coding, biology, and cybersecurity' — the specific capability areas that drove the government review in the first place. Terra is the balanced everyday model, priced at $2.50 input / $15 output per million tokens (matching GPT-5.5's old $10/$40 rate at 4x cheaper on input and half on output — see this morning's am item for the full pricing spread). Luna is the fast, low-cost tier at $1/$6. Sam Altman has framed the government-limits period publicly as an exception rather than a template: TechCrunch's June 26 quote ('restrictions shouldn't be the norm') and today's launch messaging emphasize that OpenAI complied with the June review but wants the industry to establish a lighter-weight standard going forward. New context beyond the AM's Enterprise API item: (1) the July 9 rollout is the CONSUMER rollout — ChatGPT Plus users get access tomorrow, not July 10 as originally scheduled; (2) the global preview expansion means EU / UK / APAC developers get access at the same time as US; (3) the government-review-and-clearance process for a US frontier model release is now a real thing that happens and takes ~two weeks in practice.
items.why_it_matters: The direct practical read for Gabe: tomorrow (July 9), you can use GPT-5.6 in ChatGPT Plus and via the API globally, without waiting for the July 14 Team rollout the AM item flagged. If you've been on the fence about renewing a ChatGPT Plus subscription vs. Claude Pro, you'll have a real head-to-head for the first time in three weeks — Sol vs. Fable 5 on Pro-tier consumer accounts, side-by-side, from tomorrow morning. Two structural threads worth understanding beyond that. First, the government-safety-review process for frontier model releases is now a documented, repeatable thing — not a one-off. The White House's June 2, 2026 voluntary framework got its first real-world test with GPT-5.6, and the answer is: yes, the review happens, yes, it takes roughly two weeks, and yes, the model ships afterward. That's the actual regulatory reality for US frontier labs going into the back half of 2026 — expect it to apply to Anthropic's next flagship, Gemini 3.5 Pro (see the July 17 delay item this morning), and possibly the SpaceXAI-Cursor Grok 4.5 tomorrow. If Grok 4.5 skips the review, the story becomes 'why did the safety framework only apply to OpenAI.' Second, the global-preview expansion is the practical piece for anyone doing Python work outside the US. Fable 5, GPT-5.5, Claude Sonnet 5 have all had various regional rollout stagger patterns this year — this week, OpenAI is doing what Anthropic and Google have not been doing: syncing US public and global preview on the same day. If you have collaborators in the EU, that's material for a shared toolchain choice. Concrete tomorrow-morning check: watch your ChatGPT model picker at 10am ET — Sol should appear there for Plus subscribers.
items.why_it_matters_brief: GPT-5.6 goes public globally Thursday after US Commerce Department cleared the June 26 government safety review — the first real-world test of the June 2 White House voluntary framework, and the first head-to-head Sol vs Fable 5 comparison from consumer accounts is available tomorrow morning.

items.title: Anthropic ships Claude Code and Claude Cowork inside Claude for Government Desktop in public beta on July 7, 2026 — the same commercial application delivered inside a FedRAMP High authorized environment, with hash-chained tamper-evident audit logs, standard-seats-plus-custom-tiers spend governance, hard not-to-exceed spend caps by department, and standard-agency-MDM deployment — bringing the full Claude Code + Cowork surface to federal agencies without a separate cloud-provider relationship
items.summary: Anthropic announced on July 7, 2026 (claude.com blog 'Bringing Claude Code and Claude Cowork to government,' Releasebot, Let's Data Science, Credence Wire) that Claude Code and Claude Cowork are now available in public beta through Claude for Government Desktop, built on the same commercial application shipping to enterprise and consumer customers but delivered inside a FedRAMP High authorized environment. Two application surfaces launch simultaneously: Claude Code (the CLI + IDE integrations for building and modernizing government software systems) and Claude Cowork (the desktop-file-integrated productivity surface for memo drafting, RFP reviews, casework, and deck creation). Governance and security features designed for the federal-agency context: administrators set configuration defaults and allocate spending across sub-agencies; every administrative action is recorded in a hash-chained tamper-evident audit log that admins review directly in the product; conversation history stays local to the agency-managed device; inference runs entirely inside the FedRAMP High environment; the application deploys through standard agency MDM (mobile device management) platforms. Financial model: program offices can tie AI spend to appropriated funds using standard seats, or define their own custom seat tiers with per-seat spend and model limits; usage is purchased in fixed increments with a hard not-to-exceed cap that agencies cannot exceed. Department-level admins can allocate seats and prepaid usage to sub-agencies while letting each sub-agency manage its own users. Anthropic remains the contracted and billing party — agencies do not need a separate cloud-provider (AWS Bedrock, Google Cloud) relationship to get started, which is the specific procurement friction that has slowed federal AI adoption relative to enterprise. Background context: Anthropic won FedRAMP High authorization for classified-environment workloads earlier in 2026 (Credence Wire), and Claude was already approved for FedRAMP High and DoD IL4/5 workloads via Amazon Bedrock — this launch is the first time the full Claude commercial surface (Code + Cowork) is available directly from Anthropic, not via a cloud partner.
items.why_it_matters: One structural thread for Gabe. The direct practical relevance is modest — if you don't work for or with a federal agency, you're not using Claude for Government Desktop tomorrow. But the launch matters for the industry-structure story of 2026 for a specific reason: Anthropic is now selling the exact commercial Claude Code product directly to the federal government (contracted and billed by Anthropic, not by a cloud-provider intermediary), which changes the acquisition math for both the federal AI budget and Anthropic's revenue mix. Two thread points. First, the FedRAMP High + direct-contract combination removes the specific piece of procurement friction that has kept federal AI adoption behind commercial adoption for three years. Agencies that previously needed a full AWS or Google Cloud relationship to buy Claude at scale now buy Claude Code directly, deploy it through their existing MDM infrastructure, and manage spend the way they'd manage any other software subscription. That's the concrete piece that turns 'agencies could theoretically use Claude if they set up the cloud plumbing' into 'agencies can install Claude Code tomorrow.' Second, this matters as a data point in the Anthropic-overtakes-OpenAI revenue story (see this morning's am item). The federal government AI budget is measured in tens of billions of dollars — Anthropic getting direct-contract access to that market is the specific move that supports Anthropic's guidance to $80-100B ARR by 2027 (Sacra's model). If you're calibrating how sustainable the $47B ARR gap between Anthropic and OpenAI is, this launch is the specific 'here's where the next $10B comes from' data point. Practical read for Gabe as a Python hobbyist Claude Code user: the fact that Anthropic considered the commercial Claude Code product secure enough to ship to federal agencies in a FedRAMP High environment is a real signal about the product's security posture — you're using the same application, just outside the FedRAMP compliance boundary.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Claude Code + Cowork ship into a FedRAMP High authorized government desktop with hash-chained audit logs and standard-agency-MDM deployment — Anthropic is now the direct contract party for federal AI, removing the cloud-intermediary friction that has held back federal AI adoption for three years.

revisions.change: created — Anthropic's July 7 Claude for Government Desktop public beta launch of Claude Code + Claude Cowork; FedRAMP High authorized; hash-chained tamper-evident audit logs; standard-agency-MDM deployment; hard-not-to-exceed spend caps and custom seat tiers; direct-contract model (no cloud-provider intermediary) — the substantive piece of procurement friction that has slowed federal AI adoption is now removed; complements the Anthropic-overtakes-OpenAI revenue story with a concrete 'here's where the next major TAM opens' data point

items.title: CNBC investigation on July 7, 2026 documents that Chinese open-weight AI models have taken between 30% and 46% of every week's US enterprise token traffic on OpenRouter since February 8, 2026 — up from an 11% 12-month average and 4.5% in the first half of 2025 — with Coinbase's public disclosure that it cut its AI spend nearly in half by defaulting engineers to Z.ai GLM-5.2 and Moonshot's Kimi K2.7 Code, and Vercel's finding that GLM-5.2 saw 27x daily token growth and 80x customer growth in its first week as the concrete corporate-adoption data points
items.summary: CNBC published on July 7, 2026 (CNBC, cryptobriefing, the-decoder, KuCoin flash, TechTimes, AI Weekly) an investigation confirming that Chinese open-weight AI models — specifically Z.ai's GLM-5.2, Moonshot's Kimi K2.7 Code, DeepSeek v4, and Qwen 3.5 — now account for 30% to 46% of the token usage flowing through OpenRouter, the largest US developer-facing LLM gateway. That share has stayed above 30% every week since February 8, 2026, versus an 11% average over the prior twelve months and 4.5% for the first half of 2025. KuCoin's flash cites a peak of 61% on individual weeks. Cost drives the shift: Chinese open-weight models run 60-90% cheaper than the equivalent OpenAI and Anthropic tier for equivalent-quality output on coding and general-reasoning tasks. Concrete corporate adoption data points. (1) Coinbase disclosed publicly that it cut its AI spend by nearly 50% by defaulting engineers to GLM-5.2 and Kimi K2.7 Code — TechTimes' June 28 read notes the legal-risk framing that Coinbase's CEO did not lead with in the announcement (data-residency and IP-exposure concerns from routing through Chinese-hosted inference, and secondary supply-chain-security concerns from running open weights whose training data provenance is not fully disclosed). (2) Vercel's model-usage tracker recorded GLM-5.2 as the fastest-adopted model of 2026: daily token volume grew about 27x and customer count grew about 80x in the model's first full week after the June 13 open-weight release. (3) OpenRouter's state-of-AI report tracks 100T+ tokens in aggregate through the gateway across the year to date. The trend is being framed by CEIBS' analytical read (a Chinese business school) as 'how China overtook the US in AI token usage' — the first substantive claim of a Chinese lead in a specific market segment.
items.why_it_matters: Two threads for Gabe worth understanding. First, the practical read: if you build Python side projects that hit an LLM API, GLM-5.2 through OpenRouter is now the cheapest credible frontier option. The math: GLM-5.2 at $1.40/$4.40 per million tokens versus Claude Sonnet 5 at $2/$10 (promo through August 31) versus GPT-5.6 Terra at $2.50/$15 — that's 30% cheaper than Claude and 44% cheaper than OpenAI on input, on a model that this morning's am item cited as beating GPT-5.5 on long-horizon coding benchmarks per VentureBeat. If you'd normally spend $5 on a hobby project's inference budget, the same workload costs about $2 through GLM-5.2. That's real for a hobbyist. Second, the structural read: the substantive story is that this happened without anyone in the US infrastructure ecosystem stopping it. OpenRouter is US-hosted. The developers using it are US-based. The frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic) have known about the price gap since February. And yet the 30-46% share held every week for five months. That's the specific data point that says the price/quality tradeoff is genuinely winning in the market on its own, without needing a policy push — Coinbase's 50% AI cost cut is what happens when a public company's CFO looks at the annual line item and asks the engineering team why they're spending 2x what they need to. If you build side projects the way most Python hobbyists do (small budget, personal money), you have the same incentive Coinbase's CFO does, without the enterprise-legal-risk friction. The legal-risk piece (TechTimes' Coinbase read) is worth understanding: routing through a Chinese-lab-hosted inference endpoint means your prompts and completions land on infrastructure subject to Chinese jurisdiction. For a hobbyist Python project that's probably fine; for anything containing sensitive customer data, PII, or proprietary business logic, you want either (a) the model weights self-hosted, (b) a US-based hosting provider that self-hosts the open weights, or (c) an OpenRouter-style abstraction that lets you pick the specific inference endpoint. Practical read: for coding assistance and general Python work, GLM-5.2 through OpenRouter is a defensible choice. For anything sensitive, host the weights yourself or use a US-based inference provider.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Chinese open-weight models have held 30-46% of US enterprise token share every week for five months — Coinbase publicly cut AI spend 50% moving to GLM-5.2 + Kimi K2.7 Code — the price gap (60-90% cheaper) is winning without a policy push and rewriting the calculus for hobbyists too.

items.title: Base44 launches Base 1 on June 29, 2026 — a proprietary in-house model that the Wix-owned vibe-coding platform trained on 'tens of millions of real user interactions' from its own users, explicitly targeting the generic-app aesthetic (the 'AI-slop look') that frontier models produce when generating websites — the first app-creation platform to ship its own foundation model rather than renting one from Anthropic, OpenAI, or Google, disclosed alongside a $150M ARR milestone
items.summary: Base44 launched Base 1 on June 29, 2026 (TechCrunch, The Next Web, dnyuz, Startup Fortune, News Anyway). Base44 is a San Francisco AI app-creation platform acquired by Wix in early 2026; it ships a vibe-coding surface where users describe an app in natural language and get a working Next.js/React deployment. The problem Base 1 was built to solve, in Base44 CEO's public framing: every vibe-coded app on every frontier model looks the same — generic gradient backgrounds, boilerplate sans-serif fonts, standard button treatments, the 'AI-slop' visual identity that the industry has been calling out for six months. Base44's read: the frontier labs optimize for reasoning and code correctness; nobody optimizes explicitly for design differentiation, so every downstream app inherits the same style. Base 1 is trained specifically on the design and code patterns of the tens of millions of real user interactions Base44 has captured on its platform, including the accept / reject / iterate decisions users make when a generated app doesn't look right. Model surface: general-purpose agent that holds conversation, writes code, handles multi-turn requests, tool use, and backend operations. Users can still switch between Base 1, Claude Opus 4.8, Fable 5, and OpenAI GPT-5.5 — Base 1 is the new default, not an exclusive. Base44 disclosed alongside the launch that ARR passed $150M in May 2026. Head-to-head coverage (dnyuz July 6 'We pitted Base 44's new AI model against Anthropic's to build the same website') noted Base 1 was faster on the same prompt but Base44 admits the model is 'demonstrating competitive performance' rather than beating the frontier options — this is a v1 aimed at differentiated design output, not at frontier-benchmark leadership.
items.why_it_matters: One thread for Gabe as a hobbyist Python coder. The direct practical relevance: Base44 is a vibe-coding platform, which is the specific tool category adjacent to what a Python hobbyist does when they want to ship a small web app without hand-writing every line of HTML/CSS/JS. If you've used v0, Lovable, Bolt.new, or Base44 to spin up a UI for a Python backend project, this launch is the substantive change in the space this week. Base 1 as a default model addresses the specific complaint every user of these platforms has had for a year: 'my generated app looks like every other generated app.' If Base44 has actually solved that with model-level differentiation (rather than post-hoc styling patches), the resulting apps should look meaningfully different — which is worth a look for anyone using vibe-coding tools to prototype personal projects or side businesses where visual differentiation matters. The broader structural piece is more interesting than the direct product: this is the first data point on whether an application-layer AI startup can defensibly train its own foundation model instead of renting from Anthropic / OpenAI / Google. Base44 has $150M ARR, was acquired by Wix, and clearly has the data (tens of millions of user interactions) — that's the specific set of preconditions the industry has said are required to make in-house-model training make sense. If Base 1 turns out to be genuinely competitive at the specific task Base44 cares about (differentiated app design), the pattern generalizes: any application-layer AI product with enough usage data and enough ARR to justify the training cost can plausibly train its own model tuned for its specific vertical, cutting its per-token cost by 60-90% and building a real moat against the frontier labs. That's the specific business-model piece that has been theoretical for two years. Practical read for Gabe: try Base44 on a small Python-backend project and see if Base 1 actually produces something that doesn't look like every other AI-generated app.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Base44 becomes the first vibe-coding platform to ship its own foundation model — Base 1 trained on tens of millions of user interactions to fight the 'AI-slop' look every generated app has — the first real test of whether an app-layer AI startup can train its own model defensively.

revisions.change: created — Base44 Base 1 proprietary model launch June 29 targeting 'AI-slop' generic-app aesthetic; trained on tens of millions of user interactions from the Base44 platform; $150M ARR disclosed in May; first app-layer AI startup to ship own foundation model rather than rent from frontier labs; head-to-head vs Anthropic 'faster but not beating' framing; ties to Gabe's hobbyist Python + vibe-coding surface interest

items.title: Google DeepMind ships Nano Banana 2 Lite on June 30, 2026 — a specialized image-generation variant of Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite priced at approximately $0.034 per 1,000 images with sub-four-second generation, targeting high-volume creative pipelines with 'significant leap' improvements in world knowledge, character consistency, and text rendering versus the June baseline — Google's first substantive July release while Gemini 3.5 Pro remains in preview
items.summary: Google DeepMind launched Nano Banana 2 Lite on June 30, 2026 (TechCrunch, gHacks, Neowin, BusinessToday, Coursiv, DigitalToday). Nano Banana 2 Lite is a specialized image-generation model built on Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite as its base architecture — the same lightweight foundation model that powers the free tier of Gemini image gen in the Gemini app (gemini.google.com). Positioning: designed explicitly for high-volume creative pipelines where cost and speed dominate — think auto-generation of product listing images, avatars, or downstream content-farm workflows — rather than for the highest-quality single image. Specs: sub-4-second generation latency, ~$0.034 per 1,000 images (industry-leading low price point at launch), immediate rollout to the Gemini app so end users can prompt the model directly through the web chat interface. Google's own quality framing: 'a significant leap forward' in three specific areas versus the June-baseline Nano Banana 2 — (1) world knowledge (the model correctly understands what real-world objects, people, places, and events look like), (2) character consistency (the same character prompted twice produces recognizably the same character), and (3) text rendering (in-image text like signage, book covers, and labels renders legibly and accurately). Context: Nano Banana 2 (the non-Lite parent) launched in late February 2026 as Google's fast/cheap image gen response to OpenAI's GPT-image tier; the Lite version is the specific cost-and-throughput-optimized variant for API customers. The launch is notable in the current calendar as Google DeepMind's first substantive July release — Gemini 3.5 Pro remains in limited enterprise preview with no confirmed GA date (see this morning's am item), so Nano Banana 2 Lite is what Google shipped this month while the flagship rebuild continues.
items.why_it_matters: One direct practical thread for Gabe. If you build small Python side projects that need generated images — a personal blog with generated header images, a game with generated sprites, a Streamlit app that renders diagrams from prompts — Nano Banana 2 Lite at $0.034 per 1,000 images is a genuinely cheap option. The specific math: 10,000 images for $0.34, 100,000 images for $3.40. Compared to OpenAI's DALL-E 3 tier or the Fable 5 image generation via credits, that's an order of magnitude cheaper for high-volume workflows. The catch is quality: 'Lite' means Google specifically dropped some of the quality-per-image ceiling to hit the cost-and-throughput target, so this is not the tier you use for a portfolio-quality header illustration — it's the tier you use when you need 500 category thumbnails for a Django admin panel and don't want to pay $50 for them. The three specific quality improvement axes Google called out (world knowledge, character consistency, text rendering) are the specific areas where the June baseline noticeably fell short — worth doing a small before-and-after comparison if you already had a Nano Banana pipeline in place. Second thread: this launch is a data point on Google DeepMind's July release cadence while Gemini 3.5 Pro sits in preview. Google is shipping the lightweight specialized surfaces (Flash-Lite variants, image gen) that don't require government safety review and can go directly to the Gemini app, while the flagship-tier release stays constrained. If you're calibrating 'what should I expect from Google this quarter' — the answer is: incremental Flash-Lite-derived variants and image-gen tier updates through July, and Gemini 3.5 Pro sometime after the July 17 target if it doesn't slip again. For a hobbyist, the practical implication is that Google's cheap-and-fast tier is genuinely competitive right now — worth trying via the Gemini app free tier or the Vertex AI API for small Python projects.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Sub-4-second image generation at ~$0.034 per 1,000 images — Google DeepMind's specialized Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite-based image tier is the cheapest credible image-gen option right now, useful for Python side projects that need bulk generated images at throwaway cost.

revisions.change: created — Google Nano Banana 2 Lite launched June 30 built on Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite; sub-4-second image gen at ~$0.034 per 1,000 images; three specific quality-improvement axes over the June baseline (world knowledge, character consistency, text rendering); rolled out to the Gemini app free tier and Vertex API; Google's first substantive July release while Gemini 3.5 Pro remains in preview; practical Python-hobbyist read as the cheapest credible image-gen tier for bulk workflows
